_comment: One file per procedure_code. Distilled from the matching pair in PM JAY/Standard Treatment Guideline/master/<condition>/ : _Medco.pdf (clinical + mandatory docs) + _PPD.CPD.pdf (PPD/CPD processing checklists). Filename = procedure_code. This is what the Documentation Checker, Query Predictor, and Medical Justification agents read AFTER the picker has chosen the code.
clinical_indications: Evaluation and diagnosis of patients presenting to clinic with acute or progressive weakness who are suspected of having an underlying neuromuscular disorder.

Providing diagnostic evidence that either establishes a disease etiology or focuses the differential diagnosis alongside clinical examination, electrodiagnostic, laboratory and molecular genetic testing.
Distinguishing between a necrotizing, metabolic or inflammatory myopathy in the setting of rapidly progressive muscle weakness to facilitate rapid, appropriate therapeutic management.

Identifying pathologic protein defects via immunohistochemical staining on the muscle biopsy specimen (e.g., in cases of suspected dystrophinopathy without genetic confirmation) to pave the way for genetic confirmation.

preauth.label: Clinical Notes including evaluation findings, indication of procedure and planned line of management

ppd_preauth.q: Clinical notes - detailed history, signs & symptoms, planned line of management, indication for procedure, and advice for admission?
Was Electromyography (EMG) report of patient submitted?
Was the Electromyography (EMG) report indicative of procedure?

cpd_claim.q: Were detailed Indoor Case Papers (ICPs) submitted?
Are the detailed procedure / Operative Notes available?
Was the histopathological report of muscle biopsy submitted?
Is the Discharge summary with follow-up advice at the time of discharge?

common_queries: Clinical notes missing or incomplete (lacking detailed history, signs/symptoms, planned management, or indication for procedure).
Electromyography (EMG) report not submitted or not indicative of the procedure.
Detailed Indoor Case Papers (ICPs) not submitted at claim processing.
Histopathological report of muscle biopsy not submitted at claim processing.
Detailed Procedure/Operative Notes not available at claim processing.
Discharge summary missing or lacking follow-up advice at claim processing.

clinical_key_pointers: Muscle biopsy is an important tool for the evaluation and diagnosis of patients presenting to clinic with acute or progressive weakness who are suspected of having an underlying neuromuscular disorder.
Alongside clinical examination, electrodiagnostic, laboratory and molecular genetic testing, muscle biopsy has a critical role, providing diagnostic evidence.
It can establish a disease etiology or focus the differential diagnosis.
In rapidly progressive muscle weakness, muscle biopsy is an expeditious diagnostic study to distinguish between necrotizing, metabolic or inflammatory myopathy.
Immunohistochemical staining on muscle biopsy can identify pathologic protein defects for genetic confirmation, even if initial genetic tests don't confirm dystrophinopathy.
The muscle biopsy procedure itself is fairly straightforward with little risk.
Full benefit requires involvement of a surgeon, processing laboratory, and pathologist, necessitating careful planning.

Successful muscle biopsy requires optimal cryo-processing of fresh specimens to preserve viable macromolecules for enzyme histochemistry and metabolic assays, unlike other organ biopsies requiring simple formalin preservation.

The ordering physician must orchestrate tissue collection, packaging, and processing to ensure desired testing can be performed and to avoid repeat procedures due to limited, inappropriate or poor sample quality.
Ordering clinicians should be familiar with the procedure, common pitfalls, and requirements of each team member for an optimal outcome.
Muscles traditionally chosen for biopsy include the deltoid, biceps, and quadriceps.
